mnemonics.title: HTTP Methods - CRUDS
mnemonics.content: Create=POST, Read=GET, Update=PUT, Delete=DELETE, Search=HEAD - remember CRUD but with S for Search (HEAD)

mnemonics.title: Status Code Ranges
mnemonics.content: 1xx = Informational (Just listening), 2xx = Success (Everything OK), 3xx = Redirection (Go elsewhere), 4xx = Client Error (You messed up), 5xx = Server Error (I messed up)

mnemonics.title: URL Parts - GPHQ
mnemonics.content: G=Protocol, P=Path, H=Hostname, Q=Query - Remember 'Go Pass Hot Query' to recall URL structure

acronyms.usage: Foundation protocol for web communication between clients and servers
Encrypted version of HTTP using SSL/TLS
Address of a web resource on the internet
Standard language for creating web pages
Standard format for specifying file types in HTTP
Where Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web
HTTP header-based mechanism allowing requests from different origins

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline: Client sends Request → Server processes → Server sends Response → Client receives. Label each with method, headers, status code.
Draw a box divided into sections: protocol://hostname:port/path?query#fragment - like an address letter with sender, recipient, department, and notes.
Create a number line: 100-199 (info), 200-299 (success), 300-399 (redirect), 400-499 (client error), 500-599 (server error). Color-code each range.
Draw a header as a key-value pair table with Request Headers on left side and Response Headers on right side of the HTTP transaction diagram.

analogies.analogy: HTTP is like a waiter in a restaurant - takes your order (request) to the kitchen (server) and brings back your food (response).
URL is like a home address - country (protocol), city (domain), street (hostname), house number (port), apartment (path).
Cookies are like a loyalty card - the store (server) gives you a card to remember your preferences and visit history.
A proxy server is like a hotel concierge - handles requests on your behalf, may cache information, and can filter what you receive.
GET is like reading a sign (visible to everyone, no lasting effect), while POST is like filling out an application form (private, creates/changes something).
